questions.explanation: Clustering depth measures the average overlap of micro-partitions for the clustering key. A smaller depth means less overlap and better pruning; SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION reports clustering quality.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: When is defining an explicit clustering key generally recommended?

choices.B: On very large tables (multi-terabyte) where queries frequently filter or join on specific columns and natural clustering has degraded

questions.explanation: Clustering keys benefit large tables (typically TB-scale) where query patterns consistently filter/join on certain columns and the natural ordering no longer prunes well. Small tables rarely need them.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which statement about the relationship between warehouse size and query cost is accurate?

choices.A: A larger warehouse always costs more total credits for the same query
choices.B: A larger warehouse may finish a query faster, and because billing is per-second, total cost can be similar while latency improves
choices.C: Warehouse size has no effect on performance
choices.D: Smaller warehouses always cost less total credits regardless of runtime

questions.explanation: Because billing is per-second, a larger warehouse that runs a parallelizable query proportionally faster can cost roughly the same total credits while reducing latency. The benefit depends on how well the query parallelizes.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: What is the function of the 'XL' to 'XS' resize while a warehouse is running queries?

choices.A: Running queries immediately move to the new size
choices.B: Resizing affects queued and new queries; currently running queries complete on the existing clusters
choices.D: Resizing is not allowed while running

questions.explanation: When you resize a running warehouse, queries already executing continue on the current compute; the new size applies to subsequently started (queued/new) queries.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which best describes Snowflake's approach to handling concurrency within a single-cluster warehouse?

choices.A: Unlimited queries run with no queuing
choices.B: Queries share the warehouse's resources; beyond a concurrency limit, additional queries queue
choices.C: Each query gets a dedicated cluster automatically
choices.D: Concurrency is unaffected by warehouse size

questions.explanation: A single-cluster warehouse runs concurrent queries up to its resource/concurrency limits; additional queries queue. To handle higher concurrency without queuing, use a multi-cluster warehouse (Enterprise+).

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: What does MAX_CONCURRENCY_LEVEL control on a warehouse?

choices.B: The number of concurrent statements a warehouse (cluster) attempts to run before queuing or scaling out

questions.explanation: MAX_CONCURRENCY_LEVEL sets the target number of concurrent statements per cluster. Reaching it causes queries to queue (single-cluster) or trigger additional clusters (multi-cluster), and influences resource allocation per query.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which statement about Snowflake's support for both structured and semi-structured data is correct?

choices.A: Semi-structured data must be stored in a separate system
choices.B: Structured and semi-structured data can coexist in the same table and be queried with standard SQL
choices.C: Only structured data can be queried with SQL
choices.D: Semi-structured data cannot be indexed or pruned

questions.explanation: Snowflake natively supports semi-structured data (via VARIANT) alongside structured columns in the same table, all queryable with SQL. It even maintains metadata enabling pruning on semi-structured paths in many cases.

questions.question: Which command shows all privileges granted to a specific role?

choices.B: DESCRIBE ROLE <role_name>

questions.explanation: SHOW GRANTS TO ROLE <role_name> lists the privileges granted to a role. SHOW GRANTS OF ROLE shows which users/roles have been granted that role.

questions.question: What is privilege inheritance through role hierarchy?

choices.A: A child role automatically loses the parent's privileges
choices.B: When role A is granted to role B, role B inherits all of role A's privileges
choices.C: Users inherit privileges only from the PUBLIC role

questions.explanation: Roles form a hierarchy. Granting role A to role B means B inherits A's privileges. This is why SYSADMIN, when granted custom roles, can manage all the objects those roles own.

questions.question: What does the SECURITYADMIN role have that USERADMIN does not, by default?

choices.B: The MANAGE GRANTS privilege to grant/revoke privileges globally

questions.explanation: SECURITYADMIN inherits USERADMIN (user/role management) and additionally has the MANAGE GRANTS privilege, allowing it to modify grants on any object across the account.

questions.question: A user belongs to multiple roles. How does the user access privileges from a role other than their default?

choices.A: All roles are always active simultaneously by default
choices.B: They use the active role context and can switch with USE ROLE or use secondary roles
choices.C: They must log out and back in
choices.D: Only the default role can ever be used

questions.explanation: A session has a primary active role (set with USE ROLE) plus optional secondary roles. Privileges are evaluated against the active role(s). USE SECONDARY ROLES ALL can activate all granted roles together.

questions.question: What is the recommended way to grant a custom role access so that administrators can manage its objects?

choices.A: Grant the custom role to PUBLIC
choices.B: Grant the custom role to SYSADMIN (and ultimately into the system role hierarchy)
choices.C: Grant the custom role to every user individually

questions.explanation: Best practice is to create custom roles and grant them to SYSADMIN (directly or via a parent custom role), so SYSADMIN can manage all objects in the account through the role hierarchy.

questions.question: Which feature can automatically detect and classify potentially sensitive columns (like PII) in your tables?

choices.B: Data Classification (with system tags / semantic categories)

questions.explanation: Snowflake's Data Classification analyzes column data and assigns semantic and privacy categories (e.g., identifying email, name, SSN), which can then drive tag-based masking policies for governance.

questions.question: What is the effect of applying a network policy with only blocked IP ranges and no allowed ranges?

choices.B: All IPs are allowed except those explicitly blocked

questions.explanation: If only a blocked list is specified, all addresses are allowed except the explicitly blocked ones. If an allowed list is specified, only those (minus any blocked) can connect.

questions.question: Which connectivity feature allows private, secure access to Snowflake without traversing the public internet?

choices.B: AWS PrivateLink / Azure Private Link / Google Private Service Connect

questions.explanation: Private connectivity (AWS PrivateLink, Azure Private Link, Google Private Service Connect) provides private network access to Snowflake, available with Business Critical edition and above.

questions.question: Which statement about session policies is correct?

choices.B: They define idle session timeout and other session behaviors

questions.explanation: Session policies let administrators control session parameters such as the idle session timeout, enforcing automatic session termination after inactivity for security.

questions.question: Why is key pair authentication often preferred over passwords for automated pipelines?

choices.B: It avoids storing/rotating static passwords, supports non-interactive auth, and the private key can be encrypted and rotated
choices.C: It disables MFA requirements for all users

questions.explanation: Key pair authentication uses an RSA private/public key, enabling secure non-interactive authentication for service accounts. Keys can be encrypted with a passphrase and rotated, and Snowflake supports key rotation with two keys.

questions.question: Which view or function helps identify the most expensive queries by credit/time over a period?

choices.B: SHOW WAREHOUSES

questions.explanation: The ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY view (and INFORMATION_SCHEMA.QUERY_HISTORY) provides execution details—duration, bytes scanned, warehouse—useful for identifying expensive queries to optimize.

questions.question: Which scenario most justifies enabling multi-cluster auto-scaling rather than scaling up?

choices.A: A single nightly ETL job processing huge joins
choices.B: A BI dashboard with hundreds of concurrent users running short queries at peak times

questions.explanation: High concurrency from many simultaneous users is the classic case for multi-cluster (scale out). Large, complex single queries benefit more from scaling up (a bigger warehouse).

questions.question: What is the impact of using SELECT * versus selecting only needed columns on a columnar store like Snowflake?

choices.A: No difference, since all columns are always read
choices.B: Selecting only needed columns reduces the amount of column data scanned, improving performance

questions.explanation: Because Snowflake stores data columnarly, reading fewer columns means scanning less data. Selecting only required columns reduces I/O and can improve performance and cost.

questions.question: Which is the MOST cost-effective way to provide a frequently re-run, unchanged report query with sub-second response and no warehouse cost?

choices.A: Rely on the result cache (re-run identical query within 24h on unchanged data)
choices.B: Run it on a 6XL warehouse each time
choices.C: Create a new materialized view every run

questions.explanation: If the query is identical and underlying data hasn't changed, the result cache returns results in milliseconds without a running warehouse (no compute cost), for up to 24 hours.

questions.question: Which warehouse property, if set too high, can hide concurrency issues but increase idle cost?

choices.A: MIN_CLUSTER_COUNT set high in a multi-cluster warehouse
choices.B: AUTO_SUSPEND set to 60

questions.explanation: Setting MIN_CLUSTER_COUNT high keeps multiple clusters always running, ensuring capacity but incurring cost even when demand is low. A lower minimum with auto-scaling is usually more cost-efficient.

questions.question: How can you reduce storage costs for large staging tables that don't require Fail-safe protection?

choices.A: Use permanent tables with 90-day Time Travel
choices.B: Use transient tables (no Fail-safe, minimal Time Travel)

questions.explanation: Transient tables eliminate the 7-day Fail-safe storage and can use minimal Time Travel, reducing storage costs for transient/intermediate data that can be regenerated if lost.

questions.question: What does a 'queued' state for a query in the Query Profile/monitoring usually indicate?

choices.B: The warehouse lacked available resources/slots, so the query waited before execution
choices.D: The query was canceled by the user

questions.explanation: Queuing means the query waited for compute resources to become available (e.g., concurrency limits reached). Remedies include a larger warehouse or multi-cluster scaling out for concurrency.

questions.question: Which statement about the Query Acceleration Service eligibility is correct?

choices.B: It helps queries with large scans/filters or those with outlier resource needs, identified as eligible by Snowflake
choices.C: It only works on DELETE statements

questions.explanation: QAS benefits queries with large table scans/filtering and unpredictable spikes. Snowflake estimates eligibility; you can inspect eligible queries via SYSTEM$ESTIMATE_QUERY_ACCELERATION and ACCOUNT_USAGE.

questions.question: Which credit-saving feature should you review if a warehouse shows long periods of low utilization between bursts?

choices.B: Tuning AUTO_SUSPEND and consolidating workloads

questions.explanation: Low utilization with idle gaps suggests tightening AUTO_SUSPEND so the warehouse suspends during idle periods, and possibly consolidating sporadic workloads onto fewer warehouses to avoid idle costs.

questions.question: A resource monitor is set with a quota and a 'notify at 80%, suspend at 100%' policy. What happens at 80%?

choices.B: A notification is sent but warehouses keep running

questions.explanation: At the 80% threshold with a notify action, the assigned recipients receive an alert but no suspension occurs. Suspension happens only when a suspend/suspend-immediately threshold is reached.

questions.question: Which clause in a file format object specifies how to interpret a field/column delimiter for CSV?

choices.B: FIELD_DELIMITER

questions.explanation: FIELD_DELIMITER defines the character separating fields/columns (default comma). RECORD_DELIMITER defines the row separator (default newline).

questions.question: Which file format option tells COPY to skip the first N lines, such as a header row in a CSV?

choices.B: TRIM_SPACE

questions.explanation: SKIP_HEADER = N skips the first N lines of each file (commonly 1 to skip a header row) during loading.

questions.question: Which option specifies which string values should be interpreted as SQL NULL during a load?

choices.B: NULL_IF

questions.explanation: NULL_IF lets you list string values (e.g., 'NULL', '\\N', '') that should be converted to SQL NULL when loaded into the table.

questions.question: When loading Parquet data, what is an advantage of Parquet over CSV?

choices.A: Parquet is always smaller and human-readable
choices.B: Parquet is columnar, typed, and compressed, enabling efficient, schema-aware loads
choices.D: Parquet must be uncompressed before loading

questions.explanation: Parquet is a columnar, self-describing, compressed format with typed schema and support for nested data, generally enabling more efficient and reliable loads than text-based CSV.

questions.question: Which command lists the files currently in a stage?

choices.B: LIST @stage_name

questions.explanation: LIST @stage_name (or LS) lists the files staged in the specified internal or external stage, including size and last-modified metadata.

questions.question: Which is true about loading data with COPY when MATCH_BY_COLUMN_NAME is used for semi-structured/Parquet files?

choices.A: Columns are matched by position only
choices.B: Columns in the file are matched to table columns by name, allowing schema flexibility

questions.explanation: MATCH_BY_COLUMN_NAME (CASE_INSENSITIVE or CASE_SENSITIVE) maps file fields to target columns by name rather than position, useful for Parquet/JSON loads where column order may vary.

questions.question: What does the GET command do?

choices.B: Downloads files from an internal stage to a local directory

questions.explanation: GET downloads files from a Snowflake internal stage to a local directory on the client machine. PUT does the reverse (upload).

questions.question: Which is a recommended practice to maximize COPY load parallelism for a single large dataset?

choices.B: Split the data into multiple appropriately sized files (~100–250 MB compressed) so threads can load in parallel
choices.D: Always use a single-cluster XS warehouse

questions.explanation: Splitting data into multiple files lets the warehouse's threads load them in parallel. A single huge file cannot be parallelized across threads, limiting throughput.

questions.question: Which Snowflake feature can automatically infer a table schema from staged Parquet/CSV files?

choices.B: FLATTEN

questions.explanation: The INFER_SCHEMA table function detects column definitions from staged files (Parquet, Avro, ORC, CSV, JSON), and can be combined with CREATE TABLE ... USING TEMPLATE to auto-create matching tables.

questions.question: What is a key consideration when using Snowpipe REST API auto-ingest vs. cloud notifications?

choices.B: REST API requires the client to call insertFiles to notify Snowpipe, while cloud notifications trigger automatically on file arrival
choices.C: REST API is only for unloading
choices.D: They cannot coexist with file formats

questions.explanation: Snowpipe can be triggered either by calling the REST API (insertFiles) from your application or by cloud event notifications that automatically inform Snowpipe when new files land in the stage.

questions.question: Which statement deletes rows from a target table that match a condition in a MERGE?

choices.B: WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN DELETE

questions.explanation: In a MERGE, the WHEN MATCHED THEN DELETE clause removes target rows that match the join condition (and any additional predicate). WHEN MATCHED can also UPDATE.

questions.question: Which is true about INSERT OVERWRITE?

choices.A: It appends rows to the table
choices.B: It truncates the table and then inserts the new rows in one operation

questions.explanation: INSERT OVERWRITE replaces the table's existing contents—effectively truncating then inserting the query results—within a single atomic statement.

questions.question: Which function returns the data type of a VARIANT value at runtime?

choices.B: TO_VARIANT

questions.explanation: TYPEOF returns the data type of a VARIANT expression as a string (e.g., 'INTEGER', 'VARCHAR', 'ARRAY', 'OBJECT'), useful when inspecting semi-structured data.

questions.question: When querying a VARIANT column path like data:address.city, what is the result's data type by default?

choices.B: VARIANT (which may need explicit casting to a target type)

questions.explanation: Accessing a path in a VARIANT returns a VARIANT value. To use it as a specific type (e.g., string), you typically cast it with ::STRING or use functions like TO_VARCHAR.

questions.question: Which aggregate function collects values from multiple rows into a single ARRAY?

choices.B: LISTAGG

questions.explanation: ARRAY_AGG aggregates values from a group into a single ARRAY. LISTAGG concatenates values into a delimited string, and OBJECT_AGG builds an OBJECT from key-value pairs.

questions.question: What is the difference between LISTAGG and ARRAY_AGG?

choices.B: LISTAGG returns a delimited string; ARRAY_AGG returns an ARRAY (VARIANT)
choices.C: ARRAY_AGG returns a string; LISTAGG returns an array
choices.D: Neither can be used with GROUP BY

questions.explanation: LISTAGG concatenates grouped values into a single delimited string, while ARRAY_AGG returns the grouped values as an ARRAY that can be further processed with array functions.

questions.question: Which statement about Snowflake tasks scheduling is correct?

choices.B: Tasks can be scheduled using a CRON expression or a fixed interval (e.g., every N minutes)

questions.explanation: Tasks support scheduling via SCHEDULE with either a time interval (e.g., '5 MINUTE') or a CRON expression including a time zone, and can run SQL, calls to procedures, or be child tasks in a DAG.

questions.question: In a task DAG, how is a child task triggered?

choices.B: After its predecessor (parent) task(s) complete successfully, via the AFTER clause

questions.explanation: Child tasks defined with AFTER <parent_task> run after their predecessor tasks complete. Only the root task has a schedule; downstream tasks form a dependency DAG.

questions.question: Which command is used to manually start a serverless or warehouse task immediately for testing?

choices.B: RUN TASK

questions.explanation: EXECUTE TASK manually triggers a single run of a task (and its DAG, if a root task) immediately, which is useful for testing without waiting for the schedule.

questions.question: Which type of stream tracks only INSERT operations and ignores updates/deletes, useful for append-only sources?

choices.B: Append-only stream
choices.D: Both B and C describe the same append-only behavior

questions.explanation: An append-only stream (also called insert-only for certain sources like external tables/directory tables) records only inserted rows, ignoring updates and deletes—efficient for append-only ingestion patterns.

questions.question: Which is required before you can replicate a database to another account in a different region?

choices.B: The databases/accounts must be linked under the same organization and replication enabled, then create a secondary (replica) database
choices.C: Both accounts must be Standard edition

questions.explanation: To replicate, accounts are typically part of the same organization, replication is enabled, and you create a secondary database from the primary. Cross-region/cloud replication may have additional considerations.

questions.question: What is the difference between Time Travel and Fail-safe?

choices.B: Time Travel is user-accessible recovery within the retention period; Fail-safe is a non-configurable 7-day period recoverable only by Snowflake support
choices.C: Fail-safe is user-accessible; Time Travel is not
choices.D: Time Travel lasts 7 days; Fail-safe lasts 90 days

questions.explanation: Time Travel lets users query/restore historical data within the configured retention (up to 90 days). Fail-safe is an additional, non-configurable 7-day period after Time Travel, accessible only by Snowflake for disaster recovery.

questions.question: Which objects do NOT incur Fail-safe storage costs?

choices.B: Transient and temporary tables

questions.explanation: Transient and temporary tables have no Fail-safe, so they avoid the associated 7-day storage cost. Permanent tables include Fail-safe storage.

questions.question: If you set DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 on a permanent table, what is the effect?

choices.A: It disables Time Travel for that table (no historical querying), but Fail-safe still applies
choices.B: It disables Fail-safe

questions.explanation: Setting retention to 0 disables Time Travel for the object (no AT/BEFORE or UNDROP recovery). For permanent tables, the 7-day Fail-safe still applies and cannot be disabled.

questions.question: Which best describes 'zero-copy cloning' with respect to data protection workflows?

choices.A: It duplicates data physically for backup
choices.B: It quickly creates a logical, independent copy for testing/dev or point-in-time snapshots without extra storage until changes occur
choices.D: It is slower than a full copy

questions.explanation: Zero-copy cloning instantly creates an independent logical copy sharing existing micro-partitions. It is ideal for dev/test environments and point-in-time snapshots, consuming new storage only as data diverges.

questions.question: Can you combine cloning with Time Travel to create a copy of a table as it existed in the past?

choices.A: No, cloning only works on current data
choices.B: Yes, CREATE TABLE ... CLONE ... AT/BEFORE lets you clone a historical state within retention

questions.explanation: You can clone an object at a historical point using CLONE ... AT(TIMESTAMP/OFFSET/STATEMENT) or BEFORE, as long as that point is within the Time Travel retention period.

questions.question: Which statement about sharing with a non-Snowflake customer is correct?

choices.B: The provider creates a reader account so the consumer can access shared data using a Snowflake-managed UI/compute paid by the provider
choices.C: The consumer must buy a full account

questions.explanation: Reader accounts let providers share data with consumers who lack their own Snowflake account. The provider creates and pays for the reader account's compute and manages its users.

questions.question: What governance feature ensures masking is consistently applied to all columns tagged as sensitive?

choices.B: Tag-based masking policies (attach a masking policy to a tag)

questions.explanation: Tag-based masking policies let you assign a masking policy to a tag; any column with that tag automatically inherits the masking policy, ensuring consistent protection across the account.

questions.question: Which is true about querying shared data performance?

choices.A: The consumer's queries run on the provider's warehouse
choices.B: The consumer uses their own warehouse, so performance depends on the consumer's compute
choices.C: Shared data cannot be joined with local data
choices.D: Shared data is always cached on the consumer side

questions.explanation: Consumers query shared data using their own virtual warehouses; performance is governed by the consumer's compute. Shared data can be joined freely with the consumer's own data.

questions.question: Which approach lets a provider share data so that each consumer sees only their own rows from a shared table?

choices.B: Secure views with CURRENT_ACCOUNT()/CURRENT_ROLE() logic or row access policies to filter per-consumer
choices.C: Replicating separate tables per consumer manually only

questions.explanation: Providers can use secure views referencing context functions (e.g., CURRENT_ACCOUNT) or row access policies and entitlement mapping tables so each consumer account sees only its permitted rows from a shared dataset.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: What does SnowCD (Snowflake Connectivity Diagnostic) help with?

choices.B: Diagnosing network connectivity issues between a client host and Snowflake endpoints

questions.explanation: SnowCD is a connectivity diagnostic tool that checks network access to Snowflake endpoints, helping troubleshoot firewall, proxy, and PrivateLink connectivity problems.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which connector/driver would a Python application typically use to connect to Snowflake?

choices.B: The Snowflake Connector for Python (and optionally SQLAlchemy/Snowpark)

questions.explanation: Python applications commonly use the Snowflake Connector for Python; higher-level options include the SQLAlchemy dialect and the Snowpark Python API for DataFrame-style processing.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which interface(s) can be used to administer and query Snowflake?

choices.B: Snowsight web UI, SnowSQL CLI, drivers (JDBC/ODBC), connectors, and REST APIs

questions.explanation: Snowflake can be accessed via the Snowsight web UI, SnowSQL CLI, language connectors (Python, etc.), JDBC/ODBC drivers, the SQL REST API, and Snowpark, providing broad connectivity options.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: What distinguishes Virtual Private Snowflake (VPS) from Business Critical edition?

choices.B: VPS provides a completely isolated Snowflake environment (dedicated metadata store and pool) for the highest security needs

questions.explanation: VPS is the highest edition, offering a dedicated, isolated Snowflake environment (separate from other Snowflake customers) to meet the most stringent security and regulatory requirements.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which is a correct statement about Snowflake's metadata?

choices.A: Metadata is stored within each virtual warehouse
choices.B: Metadata is managed centrally by the Cloud Services layer, enabling features like pruning and Time Travel
choices.D: Metadata is stored in the user's local cache only

questions.explanation: The Cloud Services layer centrally manages metadata (micro-partition statistics, object definitions, history), which powers query optimization, pruning, Time Travel, and zero-copy cloning.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Why can Snowflake offer zero-copy cloning so efficiently?

choices.B: Clones reference the same immutable micro-partitions via metadata pointers; no data is physically copied until changes occur
choices.C: It uses a dedicated backup warehouse

questions.explanation: Because micro-partitions are immutable and tracked by metadata, a clone simply creates new metadata pointers to the same partitions. Storage is consumed only when either copy is modified (copy-on-write).

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which statement about Snowflake's high availability is correct?

choices.A: Snowflake runs on a single server
choices.B: Snowflake is a fully managed service designed for high availability across multiple availability zones within a region
choices.D: Availability depends on the user's warehouse size

questions.explanation: Snowflake is a fully managed SaaS that distributes its services across multiple availability zones within a cloud region for resilience and high availability, with cross-region replication available for DR.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which feature provides directory-style metadata about files in a stage, enabling querying of file listings?

choices.B: External table

questions.explanation: A directory table is an implicit, queryable object layered on a stage that stores file-level metadata (path, size, last modified, etc.), often used with unstructured data and for triggering processing.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which capability supports storing and governing unstructured files (PDFs, images) in Snowflake?

choices.B: Stages with directory tables and scoped/file URLs

questions.explanation: Snowflake supports unstructured data via stages plus directory tables, and provides scoped URLs, file URLs, and pre-signed URLs (with functions like BUILD_SCOPED_FILE_URL) to access governed files.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which function generates a temporary, encoded URL to access a staged file with access enforced by Snowflake privileges?

choices.B: BUILD_SCOPED_FILE_URL

questions.explanation: BUILD_SCOPED_FILE_URL creates a scoped, time-limited URL whose access is governed by the caller's Snowflake privileges. GET_PRESIGNED_URL creates a pre-signed URL valid for a set duration that bypasses Snowflake auth.

questions.question: Which statement about applying a masking policy to a column is correct?

choices.A: A column can have multiple masking policies applied at once
choices.B: A single masking policy can be applied to many columns, but each column can have only one masking policy
choices.C: Masking policies can only be applied to numeric columns
choices.D: Masking policies physically alter stored data

questions.explanation: A masking policy is reusable across multiple columns, but a given column can have at most one masking policy. Masking is applied at query time and does not change stored data.

questions.question: Within a masking policy definition, which context function is commonly used to decide whether to mask based on who is querying?

choices.B: CURRENT_WAREHOUSE()

questions.explanation: Masking policies typically branch on CURRENT_ROLE() or invoker context functions (e.g., IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION) to return clear text for privileged roles and masked values for others.

questions.question: What does a conditional masking policy use to determine masking?

choices.B: The value of another column in the same row, in addition to context

questions.explanation: Conditional masking policies can reference another column's value (passed as an argument) to decide whether/how to mask, enabling row-aware masking decisions.

questions.question: Which privilege is needed to create masking policies and row access policies?

choices.A: CREATE MASKING POLICY / CREATE ROW ACCESS POLICY on the schema
choices.B: USAGE on the warehouse

questions.explanation: Creating policies requires the relevant CREATE <policy type> privilege on the schema (e.g., CREATE MASKING POLICY), typically managed by a security/governance role, plus APPLY to attach them.

questions.question: What is the PUBLIC role's relationship to custom roles?

choices.A: PUBLIC is the parent of all custom roles
choices.B: PUBLIC is automatically granted to all users/roles and sits at the bottom of the hierarchy

questions.explanation: PUBLIC is the lowest-level role automatically held by everyone. Objects granted to or owned by PUBLIC are accessible to all users. It has minimal privileges by default.

questions.question: What is the difference between authentication and authorization in Snowflake?

choices.B: Authentication verifies identity (login); authorization determines what actions/objects the identity can access (RBAC)

questions.explanation: Authentication confirms who you are (password, SSO, key pair, MFA), while authorization (via RBAC roles and privileges) governs what you are allowed to do once authenticated.

questions.question: Which command is used to assign a role to a user?

choices.B: ASSIGN ROLE

questions.explanation: GRANT ROLE <role_name> TO USER <user_name> assigns a role to a user. USE ROLE only activates a role within the current session; it does not grant it.

questions.question: What does setting a user's DEFAULT_ROLE accomplish?

choices.B: Specifies which already-granted role becomes active automatically when the user starts a session
choices.D: Makes the role an account admin

questions.explanation: DEFAULT_ROLE sets which of the user's granted roles is activated by default at session start. The role must already be granted to the user; the default just selects the initial active role.

questions.question: Which is a security best practice for the number of users holding ACCOUNTADMIN?

choices.B: At least two users should have it (to avoid lockout) but kept to a minimal trusted set
choices.D: It should be granted to PUBLIC

questions.explanation: Snowflake recommends having at least two ACCOUNTADMIN users (to avoid being locked out) while keeping the total minimal, using MFA, and assigning real email addresses to those accounts.

questions.question: Which feature lets you enforce that certain roles cannot be activated together in a session, supporting separation of duties?

choices.A: Secondary roles always allow all roles
choices.B: Careful role hierarchy design and restricting role grants; Snowflake evaluates privileges via active primary/secondary roles

questions.explanation: Separation of duties is implemented through deliberate role design and limiting which roles a user is granted, since privileges derive from active roles. Avoid over-granting and structure roles to prevent conflicting combined privileges.

questions.question: Which is generally the best indicator that a warehouse should be scaled UP rather than out?

choices.A: Many queries queuing due to concurrency
choices.B: Individual complex queries are slow and show heavy disk spilling
choices.C: Idle warehouse most of the day

questions.explanation: Slow individual queries with significant spilling indicate insufficient memory/CPU per query—scale up to a larger warehouse. Concurrency-driven queuing instead calls for scaling out (multi-cluster).

questions.question: Which monitoring view helps you analyze warehouse credit consumption over time?

choices.B: INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES

questions.explanation: WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY (in ACCOUNT_USAGE and INFORMATION_SCHEMA) reports credits consumed by each warehouse over time, supporting cost analysis and chargeback.

questions.question: What is a practical benefit of separating workloads onto different warehouses?

choices.B: It isolates workloads so heavy jobs don't slow down others, and enables right-sizing and cost attribution per workload
choices.D: It removes the need for roles

questions.explanation: Dedicating warehouses to specific workloads (ETL, BI, data science) isolates performance, allows independent sizing/scaling, and makes cost attribution clearer.

questions.question: Which statement about materialized view costs is accurate?

choices.B: They incur storage for the stored results plus serverless compute for automatic maintenance as base data changes
choices.C: They only incur storage, never compute
choices.D: They use the query result cache exclusively

questions.explanation: Materialized views cost storage (for materialized results) and serverless maintenance credits to keep them up to date as the underlying table changes. They suit stable data with expensive repeated queries.

questions.question: What does enabling the Search Optimization Service cost?

choices.B: Storage for the search access path plus serverless compute to build and maintain it

questions.explanation: Search Optimization adds storage for its search access paths and consumes serverless maintenance credits to build and keep them current. It pays off when many selective lookups are accelerated.

questions.question: Which factor does NOT directly increase Snowflake compute (credit) costs?

choices.B: Longer-running queries

questions.explanation: Storing more data increases storage costs, not compute credits. Compute costs are driven by warehouse size, runtime, and number of running clusters/serverless usage.

questions.question: Which setting helps prevent a single runaway query from consuming excessive credits?

choices.B: STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS

questions.explanation: STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS caps how long any query can run before being canceled, protecting against runaway queries that would otherwise consume credits indefinitely.

questions.question: When does the local disk (SSD) cache most improve performance?

choices.A: On the very first query after resuming a suspended warehouse
choices.B: On repeated queries against the same data while the warehouse stays running (warm cache)
choices.D: Only when the result cache is disabled

questions.explanation: The warehouse SSD cache benefits repeated access to the same micro-partitions while the warehouse remains running. After suspend/resume the cache is cold and must be rebuilt.

questions.question: Which is a recommended way to control costs for ad-hoc analytics by many users?

choices.B: Use a right-sized multi-cluster warehouse with auto-suspend and a resource monitor
choices.D: Run all queries on the ETL warehouse

questions.explanation: A right-sized multi-cluster warehouse handles concurrency, auto-suspend avoids idle cost, and a resource monitor caps spend—together providing cost-controlled ad-hoc analytics.

questions.question: A query scans far more partitions than expected despite a selective WHERE clause on column C. What is a likely cause?

choices.B: Data is not clustered by C, so values are spread across many micro-partitions, limiting pruning

questions.explanation: If the table isn't clustered (naturally or via a clustering key) by column C, matching values appear in many micro-partitions, so pruning can't eliminate them. Clustering or Search Optimization on C would help.

questions.question: Which is true about the relationship between a pipe and a COPY statement?

choices.A: A pipe contains no COPY logic
choices.B: A pipe wraps a COPY INTO statement that Snowpipe runs automatically as files arrive
choices.C: A pipe replaces the need for a stage
choices.D: A pipe can only unload data

questions.explanation: A pipe object encapsulates a COPY INTO <table> statement. Snowpipe executes this COPY automatically (via notifications or REST API) to load new files continuously.

questions.question: How long does Snowpipe retain load history used for duplicate detection?

choices.B: 14 days

questions.explanation: Snowpipe retains load metadata to avoid reloading the same file for 14 days. (Bulk COPY duplicate-detection metadata is retained for 64 days.)

questions.question: Which approach should you use to load data continuously with the lowest latency?

choices.B: Snowpipe with auto-ingest (or Snowpipe Streaming for row-level)

questions.explanation: Snowpipe auto-ingest provides continuous micro-batch loading shortly after files arrive. For even lower latency at the row level, Snowpipe Streaming ingests rows directly without staging files.

questions.question: What is a key difference between Snowpipe Streaming and classic Snowpipe?

choices.A: Snowpipe Streaming requires staging files first
choices.B: Snowpipe Streaming writes rows directly into Snowflake via an API, avoiding intermediate files for lower latency

questions.explanation: Snowpipe Streaming uses an SDK to write rows directly into target tables without first staging files, achieving lower latency and often lower cost for streaming row data compared to file-based classic Snowpipe.

questions.question: Which file format property would you set to handle fields enclosed in double quotes in a CSV?

choices.B: RECORD_DELIMITER

questions.explanation: FIELD_OPTIONALLY_ENCLOSED_BY specifies the character (e.g., a double quote) used to optionally enclose field values, allowing delimiters within quoted fields to be handled correctly.

questions.question: When unloading to a single Parquet file, which consideration is important?

choices.B: Use the appropriate file format type = PARQUET; very large single files may hit size limits, so partitioning may be preferable
choices.C: Parquet must be loaded as CSV first
choices.D: SINGLE = TRUE is not allowed for Parquet

questions.explanation: You can unload to Parquet by specifying TYPE = PARQUET in the file format. For very large outputs, allowing multiple files (or partitioning with PARTITION BY) is often better than forcing one huge file.

questions.question: Which COPY INTO <location> option organizes unloaded files into directories based on column values?

choices.B: CLUSTER BY

questions.explanation: The PARTITION BY clause in COPY INTO <location> writes unloaded data into a partitioned directory structure based on the specified expression, useful for data lake organization.

questions.question: What is a benefit of using external tables with partitioning over loading all external data into Snowflake?

choices.A: Faster than native tables in all cases
choices.B: You can query data in place (no ingestion/storage cost in Snowflake) and prune by partition columns

questions.explanation: External tables let you query data residing in cloud storage without loading it into Snowflake (saving storage/ingest), and partitioning enables pruning. They are read-only and metadata may need refreshing as files change.

questions.question: Which command refreshes the metadata of an external table to recognize new files?

choices.B: REFRESH STAGE

questions.explanation: ALTER EXTERNAL TABLE <name> REFRESH updates the external table's metadata to detect added/removed files. Auto-refresh via cloud notifications can also keep it current.

questions.question: Which is a valid reason to materialize a view over an external table?

choices.A: External tables cannot be queried otherwise
choices.B: A materialized view over an external table can pre-compute results and improve query performance on slow external data
choices.C: It enables writing back to external storage

questions.explanation: Creating a materialized view on an external table can significantly speed up repeated queries by pre-computing and storing results in Snowflake, mitigating the slower performance of scanning external files.

questions.question: Which clause is required to use a window function?

choices.B: OVER (...)

questions.explanation: Window functions use the OVER clause to define the window (optional PARTITION BY and ORDER BY). Unlike GROUP BY, window functions retain individual rows while computing across the window.

questions.question: What does LAG() do in a windowed query?

choices.A: Returns the value from a following row
choices.B: Returns the value from a preceding row within the window

questions.explanation: LAG() accesses a value from a previous row (offset back) within the ordered window. LEAD() accesses a value from a following row.

questions.question: Which function would you use to remove leading and trailing spaces from a string?

choices.B: REPLACE

questions.explanation: TRIM removes leading and trailing characters (spaces by default). LTRIM and RTRIM handle only the left or right side respectively.

questions.question: Which is correct about how Snowflake handles case sensitivity for unquoted identifiers?

choices.A: Unquoted identifiers are stored and resolved as lowercase
choices.B: Unquoted identifiers are stored and resolved as uppercase by default

questions.explanation: Snowflake converts unquoted identifiers to uppercase. Double-quoted identifiers preserve case and become case-sensitive, so 'mytable' and "MyTable" can differ.

questions.question: Which function concatenates strings together?

choices.B: SPLIT

questions.explanation: CONCAT(a, b, ...) or the || operator concatenates string values. CONCAT_WS adds a separator between values.

questions.question: What does the DATEADD function return?

choices.B: A new date/timestamp with a specified interval added to an input date
choices.D: A string representation of a date

questions.explanation: DATEADD(part, value, date) adds the specified interval (e.g., days, months) to a date/timestamp and returns the result. DATEDIFF computes the difference between two dates.

questions.question: Which statement creates a view that hides its definition from unauthorized users?

choices.B: CREATE HIDDEN VIEW

questions.explanation: CREATE SECURE VIEW defines a secure view whose definition is hidden from users without ownership and which bypasses certain optimizations to protect underlying data.

questions.question: Which is true about transient databases and schemas regarding contained objects?

choices.A: They can contain permanent tables with Fail-safe
choices.B: Objects created within a transient database/schema are transient by default (no Fail-safe)
choices.D: They automatically delete after one day

questions.explanation: If a database or schema is transient, tables created within it are transient by default, inheriting the no-Fail-safe characteristic. You cannot create a permanent table inside a transient container.

questions.question: Which function splits a delimited string into an ARRAY?

choices.B: FLATTEN

questions.explanation: SPLIT(string, delimiter) divides a string into an ARRAY of substrings based on the delimiter. SPLIT_PART returns a single part by index.

questions.question: Which best describes the behavior of a UDF marked as IMMUTABLE/deterministic for the same inputs?

choices.B: It returns the same output for the same inputs, enabling optimizations like caching
choices.C: It cannot be used in queries
choices.D: It must access external data each call

questions.explanation: A deterministic (immutable) function consistently returns the same result for identical inputs, allowing Snowflake to optimize and potentially reuse results. Non-deterministic functions (e.g., RANDOM) cannot be cached this way.

questions.question: Which statement about a database created from a share (inbound share) is correct?

choices.A: It consumes the consumer's storage equal to the data size
choices.B: It is a read-only database referencing the provider's data, consuming no consumer storage
choices.C: It can be fully modified by the consumer

questions.explanation: A shared (inbound) database is read-only and points to the provider's data; the consumer does not store a copy and incurs no storage cost for it—only compute when querying.

questions.question: What happens to a consumer's access if the provider removes an object from the share?

choices.A: The consumer keeps a cached copy
choices.B: The consumer immediately loses access to that object
choices.D: The object is copied to the consumer

questions.explanation: Because sharing provides live, real-time access, revoking an object from a share immediately removes the consumer's ability to query that object—no cached copy persists.

questions.question: Which is a benefit of Secure Data Sharing over traditional data exchange (e.g., file transfer)?

choices.B: No copying/ETL; consumers access live, always-current data instantly and securely

questions.explanation: Secure Data Sharing eliminates copying and ETL: consumers query the provider's live data directly, always seeing current data, with strong security and no duplicate storage.

questions.question: Which object types can a provider include in a share to expose computed logic securely?

choices.B: Secure views, secure materialized views, and secure UDFs

questions.explanation: To expose logic without revealing definitions or underlying data, providers share secure views, secure materialized views, and secure UDFs. Non-secure objects would risk exposing internal details.

questions.question: What is required to share data across cloud regions using Marketplace listings?

choices.B: Cross-cloud auto-fulfillment, which replicates the data to the target region behind the scenes
choices.C: A reader account in each region

questions.explanation: Listings support cross-cloud/region auto-fulfillment, automatically replicating the underlying data to the consumer's region so they can access it locally without the provider manually managing replication.

questions.question: Which is true about Time Travel on a table that has had its retention reduced from 30 to 1 day?

choices.A: Historical data older than the new retention becomes inaccessible going forward
choices.B: All historical data is permanently retained

questions.explanation: Reducing retention shortens how far back you can Time Travel; data beyond the new, shorter window is no longer accessible (and moves toward Fail-safe/purge). Increasing retention only affects data going forward and currently within range.

questions.question: Which command would restore a table to a previous state using Time Travel by recreating it?

choices.B: ROLLBACK

questions.explanation: You can recreate or clone a table at a prior point using CLONE with AT/BEFORE (timestamp, offset, or statement id). UNDROP only recovers a dropped object, not a logical state change.

questions.question: Which Snowflake capability supports compliance by retaining query and access history for auditing?

choices.B: ACCOUNT_USAGE views (e.g., QUERY_HISTORY, ACCESS_HISTORY, LOGIN_HISTORY)

questions.explanation: ACCOUNT_USAGE views like QUERY_HISTORY, LOGIN_HISTORY, and ACCESS_HISTORY (which tracks column-level data access) provide up to 365 days of history for auditing and compliance reporting.

questions.question: What does the ACCESS_HISTORY view provide that QUERY_HISTORY does not?

choices.B: Column-level data access lineage (which columns/objects each query read or wrote)

questions.explanation: ACCESS_HISTORY records fine-grained, column-level read/write access (data lineage) per query, supporting governance, auditing, and impact analysis beyond what QUERY_HISTORY offers.

questions.question: Which feature would you use to ensure sensitive data shared with a partner is masked unless they have an authorized role?

choices.B: Masking policy applied to the shared columns (in combination with secure views)

questions.explanation: Applying masking policies to columns (often via secure views in the share) ensures consumers see masked values unless their context/role is authorized, preserving privacy in data sharing scenarios.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which statement about Snowflake's storage compression is correct?

choices.A: Customers choose and manage the compression algorithm
choices.B: Snowflake automatically compresses data and customers are billed on the compressed size
choices.D: Compression only applies to semi-structured data

questions.explanation: Snowflake automatically applies columnar compression to all stored data; customers are billed based on the compressed storage size, and the compression is fully managed by Snowflake.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which is the correct order of object containment in Snowflake?

choices.B: Account > Database > Schema > Table

questions.explanation: The containment hierarchy is Account > Database > Schema > Table (and other schema-level objects like views, stages, and pipes).

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: What is an account in Snowflake?

choices.B: An independent Snowflake deployment with its own URL, users, roles, databases, and warehouses

questions.explanation: A Snowflake account is an isolated instance with its own identifier/URL, hosting users, roles, databases, warehouses, and configuration. Multiple accounts can belong to one organization.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which scenario best illustrates the benefit of separating storage and compute?

choices.A: You must shut down all queries to add storage
choices.B: An ETL warehouse and a BI warehouse query the same tables concurrently and are sized/billed independently
choices.C: Storage scales only when compute scales
choices.D: Only one warehouse can run at a time

questions.explanation: Separation of storage and compute lets multiple independent warehouses access the same shared data simultaneously, each sized and billed separately—enabling workload isolation without data duplication.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which best describes 'pruning' in Snowflake query processing?

choices.B: Skipping micro-partitions that cannot contain rows matching the query filters, using partition metadata
choices.C: Removing unused columns from a table

questions.explanation: Pruning uses per-micro-partition metadata (min/max, etc.) to skip partitions that cannot match the query's predicates, reducing the amount of data scanned and improving performance.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which factor most influences how well Snowflake can prune micro-partitions for a query?

choices.B: How well the table's data is clustered/ordered relative to the filtered columns

questions.explanation: Pruning effectiveness depends on data organization: if the filtered column's values are well-clustered, fewer partitions need scanning. Poor clustering spreads values across many partitions, reducing pruning.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which Snowflake object is schema-level rather than account-level?

choices.B: Table

questions.explanation: Tables (and views, stages, pipes, sequences, etc.) are schema-level objects. Warehouses, roles, and users are account-level objects.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: What is the role of the optimizer in the Cloud Services layer?

choices.B: To parse SQL and generate an efficient execution plan, including pruning decisions, before dispatching to a warehouse

questions.explanation: The Cloud Services optimizer parses and compiles SQL, applies cost-based optimization (join ordering, pruning, etc.), and produces a plan that the virtual warehouse executes.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which is true about a suspended virtual warehouse?

choices.B: It consumes no compute credits while suspended

questions.explanation: A suspended warehouse consumes no compute credits. Storage costs continue (they're independent of warehouses), but you only pay compute when a warehouse is running.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which feature allows running Python, Java, or Scala code inside Snowflake's secure sandbox close to the data?

choices.B: Snowpark (including Snowpark-optimized warehouses and UDFs/procedures)

questions.explanation: Snowpark runs Python/Java/Scala logic within Snowflake's secure sandbox. Snowpark-optimized warehouses provide additional memory for demanding workloads like ML, keeping computation close to the data.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which best describes an organization in Snowflake?

choices.B: A first-class entity that groups and manages multiple Snowflake accounts under unified administration

questions.explanation: An organization links multiple Snowflake accounts for centralized management, enabling cross-account features like replication, failover, and consolidated usage via ORGANIZATION_USAGE, managed by ORGADMIN.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which statement about Snowflake's support for ANSI SQL is correct?

choices.A: Snowflake uses a proprietary query language only
choices.B: Snowflake supports standard ANSI SQL with extensions for semi-structured data and other features

questions.explanation: Snowflake supports standard ANSI SQL plus extensions (e.g., FLATTEN, VARIANT path access, QUALIFY), making it accessible to SQL practitioners while adding modern capabilities.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which cache would help a dashboard that repeatedly runs the exact same query on unchanged data?

choices.B: Result cache

questions.explanation: Identical queries on unchanged data are served from the result cache (up to 24 hours), returning instantly without using a warehouse—ideal for repeated dashboard queries.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Why is Snowflake described as a 'data cloud' beyond just a data warehouse?

choices.B: Because it unifies data warehousing, data lakes, data sharing/collaboration, data engineering, apps, and AI/ML on one platform
choices.C: Because it runs only on one cloud
choices.D: Because it replaces all cloud providers

questions.explanation: The 'AI Data Cloud' encompasses multiple workloads—data warehouse, data lake, engineering, sharing/collaboration, applications, and AI/ML (Cortex)—across multiple clouds, going beyond a traditional data warehouse.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which is true about how many schemas a database can contain?

choices.B: Many schemas, each containing tables and other objects
choices.D: Schemas are not allowed in databases

questions.explanation: A database can contain many schemas. By default it includes PUBLIC and INFORMATION_SCHEMA, and you can create additional schemas to organize objects.

questions.question: Which command activates a different role within the current session?

choices.B: USE ROLE

questions.explanation: USE ROLE <role_name> switches the active (primary) role for the current session, provided the role has been granted to the user.

questions.question: Which is true about secondary roles in a session?

choices.B: They allow a session to use privileges from additional granted roles alongside the primary role
choices.C: They are always all roles by default
choices.D: They can only be system roles

questions.explanation: Secondary roles (USE SECONDARY ROLES ALL) let a session combine privileges from multiple granted roles in addition to the primary role, useful when queries span objects owned by different roles.

questions.question: Which privilege allows a role to read data from a table?

choices.B: USAGE

questions.explanation: The SELECT privilege on a table (plus USAGE on the parent database and schema) allows a role to query the table's data.

questions.question: What is the minimum set of privileges a role needs to query a table in another schema?

choices.B: USAGE on the database, USAGE on the schema, and SELECT on the table

questions.explanation: Privileges are hierarchical: to query the table you need USAGE to traverse the database and schema containers plus SELECT on the table itself.

questions.question: Which is a recommended practice for managing access to many objects with minimal grants?

choices.A: Grant privileges directly to each user
choices.B: Use functional roles and future grants, and build a role hierarchy

questions.explanation: Best practice is to design functional/access roles, build a hierarchy, and use future grants so newly created objects automatically inherit privileges—minimizing repetitive manual grants.

questions.question: Which is true about MFA enrollment in Snowflake?

choices.A: It is enforced for all users automatically
choices.B: Users self-enroll (via Duo), and admins can require/strongly encourage it, especially for privileged roles

questions.explanation: Snowflake MFA (powered by Duo) is available to all users who self-enroll. Administrators are advised to require MFA particularly for ACCOUNTADMIN and other privileged users for stronger security.

questions.question: Which is the purpose of the MANAGE GRANTS privilege?

choices.B: To grant or revoke privileges on objects the role does not own

questions.explanation: MANAGE GRANTS allows a role (like SECURITYADMIN) to grant and revoke privileges on objects across the account, even those it does not own, centralizing grant administration.

questions.question: Which approach helps comply with a requirement that data engineers can manage pipelines but cannot view production PII?

choices.B: Use RBAC to grant pipeline/object privileges while applying masking policies that mask PII for the engineering role
choices.D: Store PII in a separate account only

questions.explanation: Combine RBAC (granting needed operational privileges) with dynamic data masking so the engineering role can manage pipelines but sees masked PII values, satisfying least-privilege and data-protection requirements.

questions.question: Which command would you use to see what roles have been granted to a user?

choices.B: SHOW ROLES

questions.explanation: SHOW GRANTS TO USER <user_name> lists the roles granted to that user. SHOW GRANTS TO ROLE shows privileges of a role.

questions.question: What is a recommended pattern for separating object-access roles from functional (business) roles?

choices.A: Put all privileges in one role
choices.B: Create access roles holding object privileges, grant them to functional roles, and grant functional roles to users

questions.explanation: A common best practice separates 'access roles' (which hold specific object privileges) from 'functional roles' (aligned to job functions). Access roles are granted to functional roles, which are granted to users—improving manageability.

questions.question: Which factor is NOT part of a virtual warehouse's compute resources?

choices.B: Memory

questions.explanation: A warehouse provides CPU, memory, and temporary local SSD storage for query processing. Long-term table storage is part of the separate Database Storage layer, not the warehouse.

questions.question: Which scenario indicates that increasing the warehouse size will likely NOT help much?

choices.A: A query spilling heavily to remote storage
choices.B: A simple, highly selective single-row lookup that already prunes well and runs in milliseconds
choices.C: A large aggregation running out of memory
choices.D: A complex multi-join query bottlenecked on memory

questions.explanation: A tiny, well-pruned lookup is not compute-bound, so a bigger warehouse adds cost without meaningful speedup. Larger warehouses help compute-heavy queries that spill or need more parallelism/memory.

questions.question: Which is a benefit of setting AUTO_SUSPEND to a reasonable value like 60 seconds for an interactive warehouse?

choices.A: It keeps the cache warm forever
choices.B: It balances avoiding idle costs with retaining the cache briefly between bursts of activity

questions.explanation: A modest AUTO_SUSPEND (e.g., 60s) suspends quickly when truly idle while keeping the warehouse warm during short gaps in interactive use, balancing cost savings with cache reuse.

questions.question: Which combination would BEST optimize a workload of many concurrent, selective lookups on a huge table?

choices.A: A single XL single-cluster warehouse only
choices.B: Search Optimization Service on the lookup columns plus a multi-cluster warehouse for concurrency
choices.D: Clustering key on a low-cardinality column only

questions.explanation: Selective point lookups benefit from Search Optimization (fast pruning), while many concurrent users benefit from a multi-cluster warehouse to avoid queuing—together optimizing both per-query and concurrency performance cost-effectively.

questions.question: Which metric would you check to see if a warehouse is frequently queuing queries?

choices.B: Queued overload time / queued provisioning time in query history or warehouse load

questions.explanation: Query history and the warehouse load chart expose queuing metrics (queued overload/provisioning time). High queuing indicates a need to scale out (multi-cluster) or otherwise add capacity.

questions.question: Which is true about the cost relationship of a multi-cluster warehouse?

choices.A: It always costs the same as a single-cluster warehouse
choices.B: You pay for each active cluster's credits; more running clusters means proportionally more credits

questions.explanation: A multi-cluster warehouse bills credits for each running cluster at the warehouse's size rate. Auto-scaling adds clusters (and cost) under load and removes them when demand drops to control spend.

questions.question: Which is the most direct way to identify which queries scanned the most bytes last week?

choices.B: SHOW TABLES

questions.explanation: Querying QUERY_HISTORY and sorting by BYTES_SCANNED (and execution time) reveals the heaviest queries, which are prime candidates for optimization (clustering, rewriting, or right-sizing).

questions.question: Which approach reduces cost for an infrequently used but occasionally heavy reporting workload?

choices.A: Keep a large warehouse always running
choices.B: Use a warehouse with auto-suspend/auto-resume so it only runs (and bills) when reports are executed
choices.D: Run reports on the smallest warehouse regardless of need

questions.explanation: Auto-suspend/auto-resume ensures the warehouse is only consuming credits during actual report execution, avoiding idle cost for an intermittent workload while still being available on demand.

questions.question: What is the effect of the result cache on cost for repeated identical queries?

choices.B: It can serve results without a warehouse, avoiding compute cost for those repeats
choices.C: It has no effect on cost

questions.explanation: Result cache hits return data from Cloud Services without running a warehouse, so repeated identical queries on unchanged data incur no compute cost.

questions.question: A team complains their queries slow down every morning when the warehouse first resumes. What is the likely cause and remedy?

choices.A: Storage limit reached; buy more storage
choices.B: Cold SSD cache after resume; the first queries rebuild the cache. Consider a keep-warm strategy or accept brief warm-up

questions.explanation: After suspension, the local SSD cache is cleared, so the first queries after resume run colder. Options include scheduling a warm-up query, increasing AUTO_SUSPEND, or accepting the brief initial slowdown.

questions.question: Which is true about loading a CSV with extra columns not present in the target table?

choices.A: It always succeeds and ignores extras
choices.B: You can select specific columns via a transformation, or it may error depending on options like ERROR_ON_COLUMN_COUNT_MISMATCH
choices.C: Extra columns are added to the table automatically

questions.explanation: By default, ERROR_ON_COLUMN_COUNT_MISMATCH causes an error if the number of columns differs. You can disable it or use a SELECT transformation in COPY to map only the desired columns.

questions.question: Which strategy avoids reloading already-processed files while still detecting genuinely new files?

choices.B: Rely on Snowflake's default load history (skip already-loaded files) and avoid FORCE unless intentional
choices.C: Delete and recreate the table each load

questions.explanation: Snowflake's default behavior tracks load history and skips files already loaded, so simply running COPY again loads only new files. FORCE = TRUE overrides this and risks duplicates.

questions.question: Which is required to load data from a private S3 bucket using an external stage?

choices.B: Credentials or a storage integration granting Snowflake access to the bucket

questions.explanation: To read from a private bucket, Snowflake needs authorization—either inline credentials or, preferably, a storage integration that trusts a cloud IAM role granting access to the bucket.

questions.question: Which is a valid way to handle a load where some rows have data errors but you want to load the good rows?

choices.B: ON_ERROR = CONTINUE to skip bad rows and load the rest

questions.explanation: ON_ERROR = CONTINUE skips individual erroneous rows and loads the remaining valid rows. You can then review skipped rows via COPY_HISTORY/validation to address the errors.

questions.question: Which command can validate and return all errors for files without loading them?

choices.B: LIST @stage

questions.explanation: Running COPY with VALIDATION_MODE = RETURN_ALL_ERRORS validates the staged files and returns all errors encountered, without loading any data—useful for pre-load checks.

questions.question: What is a benefit of using a named stage over a table or user stage?

choices.B: Named stages are flexible, reusable across tables/users, and can have associated file formats and access controls
choices.C: Named stages are only for unloading

questions.explanation: A named (internal or external) stage is a first-class object that can be reused across multiple tables and users, can have a default file format, and supports granular privileges—more flexible than table or user stages.

questions.question: Which is true about loading JSON where each file contains one large array of records?

choices.B: Use STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY to load each array element as a separate row
choices.C: It loads as a single row only and cannot be split
choices.D: JSON arrays must be converted to CSV first

questions.explanation: The STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY file format option removes the outer array brackets so each element becomes its own row in the VARIANT column, rather than loading the entire array as one row.

questions.question: Which approach is appropriate when files arrive irregularly and you want hands-off, near-real-time loading without managing a warehouse?

choices.A: Scheduled bulk COPY on a large warehouse
choices.B: Snowpipe auto-ingest (serverless)

questions.explanation: Snowpipe auto-ingest is serverless and event-driven, automatically loading files shortly after they arrive without you provisioning or managing a virtual warehouse—ideal for irregular, near-real-time ingestion.

questions.question: Which is the correct general direction of the PUT and GET commands?

choices.B: PUT uploads to a stage, GET downloads from a stage

questions.explanation: PUT uploads local files to an internal stage; GET downloads files from an internal stage to the local file system. Neither works on external stages.

questions.question: Which statement about PUT and external stages is correct?

choices.A: PUT can upload directly to external stages
choices.B: PUT only works with internal stages; for external stages you upload via the cloud provider's tools

questions.explanation: PUT (and GET) operate only on internal stages. To place files in an external stage's underlying bucket, you use the cloud provider's upload mechanisms (e.g., AWS CLI, Azure tools).

questions.question: Which statement about a CTE (Common Table Expression) is correct?

choices.B: It defines a named, temporary result set within a single query using WITH

questions.explanation: A CTE, introduced with WITH, defines a named temporary result set usable within the scope of a single statement, improving readability and enabling recursion.

questions.question: Which is true about deterministic vs. non-deterministic functions and the result cache?

choices.A: Non-deterministic functions (e.g., CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, RANDOM) can prevent result cache reuse
choices.B: All functions allow result caching
choices.D: Caching is unrelated to function determinism

questions.explanation: Queries using non-deterministic functions like RANDOM() or certain time functions may not be eligible for result cache reuse because results can change between executions.

questions.question: Which function returns the number of elements in an ARRAY?

choices.B: LENGTH

questions.explanation: ARRAY_SIZE returns the number of elements in an ARRAY. LENGTH works on strings/binary, and COUNT is an aggregate over rows.

questions.question: Which approach correctly extracts a nested value and casts it to a number from a VARIANT column v?

choices.B: NUMBER(v.amount)

questions.explanation: v:amount accesses the 'amount' path in the VARIANT, and ::NUMBER casts it to a numeric type. This colon path notation with a cast is the idiomatic way to extract typed values from semi-structured data.

questions.question: Which DDL command changes a table's clustering key?

choices.B: CREATE INDEX

questions.explanation: ALTER TABLE <name> CLUSTER BY (expr, ...) defines or changes a table's clustering key. Snowflake does not use traditional CREATE INDEX statements.

questions.question: Which is the correct behavior of a standard stream after the source table is fully consumed by a DML?

choices.B: The stream's offset advances; querying it again shows no rows until new changes occur
choices.C: The stream keeps showing the same rows forever

questions.explanation: Once consumed within a committed DML transaction, the stream advances its offset; subsequent reads return no change rows until further DML modifies the source table.

questions.question: Which function returns the position of a substring within a string?

choices.B: SUBSTRING

questions.explanation: POSITION(substring IN string) (or CHARINDEX) returns the 1-based index where the substring first appears, or 0 if not found.

questions.question: Which clause makes a MERGE handle rows present in the source but not the target?

choices.B: WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT

questions.explanation: WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT inserts source rows that have no matching target row, the 'insert new records' part of an upsert.

questions.question: Which best describes the GROUP BY clause?

choices.B: It groups rows sharing values in specified columns so aggregates compute per group

questions.explanation: GROUP BY partitions rows into groups based on the listed expressions, allowing aggregate functions (SUM, COUNT, etc.) to be computed per group.

questions.question: Which is true about GROUP BY ALL in Snowflake?

choices.A: It groups by every row individually
choices.B: It automatically groups by all non-aggregated columns in the SELECT list

questions.explanation: GROUP BY ALL is a convenience that groups by all SELECT-list expressions that are not aggregate functions, reducing the need to list grouping columns explicitly.

questions.question: Which is the result type of PARSE_JSON('{"a":1}')?

choices.B: VARIANT (OBJECT)

questions.explanation: PARSE_JSON returns a VARIANT; for a JSON object input the underlying type is OBJECT, which can be navigated with path notation.

questions.question: Which is a correct way to convert a VARIANT array into rows with their index?

choices.A: Use LATERAL FLATTEN and reference the value and index columns
choices.B: Use PIVOT

questions.explanation: LATERAL FLATTEN(input => v) returns columns including VALUE and INDEX for each array element, letting you expand an array into rows with positional index.

questions.question: Which is true about views referencing other views?

choices.B: Views can be layered (a view can select from other views)
choices.C: Only secure views can reference views
choices.D: Views can only reference base tables

questions.explanation: Views can be built on top of other views, enabling layered abstractions. Each layer's query is resolved at execution time.

questions.question: Which statement about Snowflake's handling of NULLs in aggregate functions is correct?

choices.B: Most aggregates (e.g., SUM, AVG, COUNT(col)) ignore NULL values
choices.C: Aggregates fail if any NULL is present

questions.explanation: Aggregate functions like SUM, AVG, and COUNT(column) ignore NULLs. COUNT(*) counts all rows including those with NULLs. This is standard SQL behavior.

questions.question: Which is the correct way to alias a column in a SELECT?

choices.B: SELECT ALIAS(col)

questions.explanation: You alias a column with 'col AS new_name' (or simply 'col new_name'). The AS keyword is optional in Snowflake.

questions.question: Which is true about a clone's independence from its source after creation?

choices.A: Changes to the clone affect the source
choices.B: The clone and source are independent; modifying one does not affect the other

questions.explanation: After cloning, the clone is fully independent. Thanks to copy-on-write, changes to either the clone or source are isolated and do not affect the other.

questions.question: Which best explains why dropping and recreating a large table can affect Time Travel/Fail-safe storage costs?

choices.B: The dropped version remains recoverable in Time Travel/Fail-safe, temporarily retaining storage for both old and new data

questions.explanation: After dropping, the old data persists through Time Travel and Fail-safe windows. If you also load new data, you temporarily pay storage for both, until the old version's retention/Fail-safe expires.

questions.question: Which is true about replicating shares vs. databases?

choices.A: Only databases can be replicated; shares are local
choices.B: Both databases and account objects can be part of replication/failover groups for cross-region availability
choices.C: Shares cannot be used after replication

questions.explanation: Replication and failover groups can include databases and other account objects (roles, warehouses metadata, shares, etc.), enabling cross-region/cloud business continuity and consistent sharing setups.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a failover group (vs. a replication group)?

choices.A: Only one-way copy with no failover
choices.B: To enable failover/failback of the replicated objects to a secondary account for disaster recovery

questions.explanation: A failover group (Business Critical+) extends replication by allowing you to promote the secondary to primary (failover) and later fail back, supporting disaster recovery and business continuity.

questions.question: Which is true about encryption key management responsibilities by default (without Tri-Secret Secure)?

choices.B: Snowflake fully manages encryption keys automatically

questions.explanation: By default, Snowflake manages all encryption keys via its hierarchical key model with automatic rotation. Tri-Secret Secure (Business Critical) adds a customer-managed key into the hierarchy.

questions.question: With Tri-Secret Secure, what can a customer do that mitigates risk if their key is compromised or access must be revoked?

choices.B: Disable/revoke their customer-managed key, rendering data inaccessible (a 'kill switch')
